pages.raw_text:
The Review gave no space what
ever yesterday morning to the fol-
lowing communication:


TO THE WAGE FARMERS: To-
morrow the voters of Spokane will
select candidates for mayor and
councilmen. You will have a large
part in selecting these candidates.


Mr.. Fassett in his speech at the
First Presbyterian church last
Thursday. said:


"In selecting city officials you
should do the same as you would
do in selecting an employe for
|yourself-study your man., know
him, and select the man you be-
lieve to be the strongest and most
worthy"


And he might we'll have added
that you should study what they
stand for and who is backing them.
You might also look to the candi-
dates record.


II you take Mr.. Fassett's advice
1t is not at all probable that Fas-
sett will be nominated.


The Spokesman-Review is trying
hard to elect Fassett. Did the
Spokesman-Review ever do any
thing except from selfish motives?


Has 1t not at every opportunity
struck blow at the worker? In
reporting labor news has 1t not al
ways twisted the truth around in
such q manner as to show the
worker in the worst possible light?
The Review knows that in this
contest Hutchinson is stronger
man than Fassett. 1t knows very
we'll, too, that. ONO stands no
show of being nominated. Yet 1t
hopes by trickery to have Fassett
nominated. 1t is trying to confuse
the voter by claiming that the fight
is between ONO and Fassett and
in that way draw labor votes from
Hutchinson to ONO.


Do not let their ruse succeed. II
you want Fassett for mayor by all
means vote for him, but II you do
not unite on Hutchinson Fassett
will surely be elected as ONO can
not draw enough votes from the
business men to defeat him.


Outside of very few workers
who have swallowed his dope about
his being labor's champion. ONO
can only look to the saloons and
dives for his support and the day
of saloon control in Spokane is
past.


On the other hand Hutchinson
has the confidence of all classes
and can draw enough votes, which
when combined with the workers
votes, will elect him. 1t may be
true that Mr.. Fassett, II eletced,
would be a good mayor for the
workers, but when the Review and
the Washington Water Power Co.
both back candidate the evidence
is all against such probability.


The backing of either. should
convince you that he Is not the
man you want for mayor.


The Review and the Washington
Water Power Co.!


Ye gods! What a combination to
ask for a workers vote!


But then the Review or the
Washington Water Power Co. has
sufficient gall to ask for anything.


On the contrary, consider Hutch
inson's record Of public service
since 1891. Always in support Of
those measures that were for the
public good. many of which were
strictly for the benefit of the wage
Garner; introducing many such
bills himself and succeeding In
passing q number of them.
There is an old saying that the
worker is ever ready to cruelty his
